publications.editedComment: Four doors, all labeled. The picker can use every available image, recently generated art, one collection, or several collections. Single mode offers one select; multiple mode opens a grid, marks chosen cards, and can show removable preview badges. Clear returns everything to `all`. Every change emits the mode, one collection id when relevant, the selected id array when relevant, and a combined change payload. On mount it initializes collection data without remote image hydration. Empty shelves are reported plainly. Nothing vanishes into the back room: selection state, visibility, maturity, and image counts remain on the receipt.

This picker turns an art library into four clear merchandising sources: all art, generated art, one collection, or multiple collections. It derives labels from title, label, name, or collection id, and its metadata line states image count, public/private visibility, and safe/mature rating. Multi-select cards toggle ids and preview badges remove them individually; Clear resets the source to all art. The component emits normalized mode, single-id, multi-id, and combined change events so a parent can build the actual product set. A useful catalog control: source choice first, collection detail second, no hidden inventory mutation.

Four happy places for chaos! :) Header, Sheet, Page, and Hand each choose `off`, `behind`, or `front`, and Reset restores the surface defaults. Below them, every catalog effect becomes a toggle button with its icon, active reveal text, tooltip, and an `interactive` chip when it captures clicks. The header counts active effects and total effects, while Manage builds links to the animation manager. Hovering explains an effect before activation; pressed state mirrors the store. Effects can stack and follow between pages. Responsible sparkle means the layers and click-blocking warnings are visible before the confetti starts. Have a productive forever!

The panel offers control, and then reflects exactly how fragile control is. Header, Sheet, Page, and Hand can each be dark, behind the surface, or in front of it. The store remembers those placements, counts active effects, and supplies every effect’s label, reveal phrase, color, icon, tooltip, and click-blocking flag. A hover mirror names what the effect does; an active button changes its face; Reset returns the surfaces to their defaults. Several effects may occupy the same page. The useful truth is not that chaos disappears, but that its layer and ability to capture input are declared before you invite it in.

The manifest looks into the database, and the database is not allowed to erase what it sees. Dry Run loads `/wonderlab-components.json` and asks the reconcile API for creates, updates, unchanged rows, missing records, and conflicts. Apply is enabled only after that dry run, with the same saved manifest and zero conflicts. It may create missing Components, synchronize canonical identity fields, or change the discovery flag for database-only records. Notes, artwork, reactions, and curator-selected status remain intact; deletion is outside the mirror. Counts and expandable evidence keep every proposed change visible before the second request is made.

The lamp stays on between inspection and repair. Dry Run fetches the generated manifest without cache, validates its entries, and posts it to `/api/components/reconcile`. The result separates creates, updates, unchanged identities, records missing from the manifest, and conflicts. Apply Safe Changes remains disabled unless the displayed result is a dry run, the manifest is still held, and conflicts equal zero. Applying reuses that exact manifest. Database-only rows are preserved and merely marked undiscovered; reactions and curator status are not altered. Error and success messages remain above the evidence lists. A careful keeper checks the glass before carrying the flame.

This page does one thing and does not decorate the answer. It labels the view Global Leaderboard, passes `achievementStore.highClickScores` into `leaderboard-table`, and tells the table to read `clickRecord` under the label Click Record. On mount, it fetches high-click scores only when the store does not already contain them. There is no local sorting, mutation, pagination, or invented score math in this wrapper. The store owns the data and the shared table owns presentation. Small component. Clear contract. Good perch.

Global Leaderboard, and one shared table, and one computed link to `highClickScores`, and one score key named `clickRecord`, and one label named Click Record. When the page mounts and the store is empty, it fetches the scores; when scores already exist, it does not request them again. The wrapper does not duplicate ranking logic and it does not transform the rows and it does not own the achievement data. The connection is the feature: achievement store to computed rows, rows to leaderboard table, table to the people comparing clicks together.

The card’s spread is unusually legible. Its title falls back from curated title to component name to numeric id. A canonical status determines the icon, badge, border, and labels for Unreviewed, Working, Needs context, Building, Broken, Retired, or Preview unsupported. Optional positions reveal notes, average rating and count, review count, last-reviewed date, folder, id, update date, preview mode, discovery warning, art id, and raw debug data. Selection updates the Component store and emits the record; Edit emits the id; Copy writes the component name. The reading changes appearance, never the underlying facts.
